nativeRange.habitat: Fast-flowing, highly oxygenated clear-water rivers with rocky substrate and scattered boulders; periphyton-covered rock surfaces provide primary grazing area
nativeRange.biotope: Xingu / Tapajós river rapids (white-water and clear-water)

decorPreferences: smooth rounded boulders and flat rocks providing territory caves
large pieces of driftwood (bogwood) for grazing and shelter
sandy or fine gravel substrate between rock formations
moderate to strong water flow via powerheads or circulation pumps
dimmer lighting or shaded areas with rocks

diet.notes: Herbivore-leaning omnivore. Primary diet is biofilm and algae grazed from rock surfaces; supplement with algae wafers, spirulina wafers, blanched courgette (zucchini), cucumber, and spinach. Protein component is important — offer meaty foods such as sinking carnivore pellets, frozen bloodworm, and frozen brine shrimp 2–3 times per week. Avoid over-reliance on algae alone; insufficient protein leads to poor condition. Driftwood in the tank provides beneficial cellulose. Live tubifex and blackworm are eagerly accepted as treats.

goodWith: medium to large South American cichlids (Geophagus, Uaru)
other medium-to-large plecos kept in adequately sized tank with separate cave territories
mid-water shoaling fish that do not compete for bottom space
avoidWith: other Baryancistrus or territorial plecos unless tank is very large with multiple defined territories
small or nano fish (may be disturbed by the pleco's size and activity)
aggressive fish that nip at the pleco's soft-rayed fins
fish requiring cool water (incompatible temperature range)
species sensitive to strong water flow

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase image for Baryancistrus xanthellus (the formally described Gold Nugget Pleco species); check availability and licence before use.

summary: A striking loricariid catfish from Brazil's Rio Xingu and Tapajós, identifiable by dark body covered in bright yellow-gold spots with yellow-tipped dorsal and caudal fins. Sold under multiple L-numbers (L18, L81, L85, L177); B. xanthellus is the formally described species. Intermediate care due to high oxygen, warm temperature, and territorial nature.
careNotes: The Gold Nugget Pleco is one of the most visually stunning plecos in the hobby, distinguished by its jet-black or dark brown body peppered with golden-yellow polka dots and matching yellow fin margins. It originates from the high-energy rapids of the Rio Xingu and Rio Tapajós in Brazil, and replicating this environment in the aquarium is key to long-term success. Water must be warm (26–30 °C), well-oxygenated, and moderately soft to neutral (pH 6.0–7.5, gH 2–12). Strong water movement via powerheads or high-flow filtration is essential; stagnant or low-oxygen conditions are poorly tolerated and quickly cause health decline. A minimum tank of 300 L (approximately 150 cm footprint) is recommended for a single adult, which can reach 25–30 cm. Multiple specimens require a substantially larger tank with clearly demarcated cave territories among the rocks — males are particularly intolerant of conspecifics. Smooth rounded boulders, flat slate rocks, and large pieces of driftwood should be arranged to create multiple distinct cave hideouts. Feeding requires attention: although an avid algae-grazer, this species needs regular meaty protein supplements (frozen bloodworm, brine shrimp, sinking carnivore pellets) to thrive. Copper-based medications must be avoided, as with all loricariids. The four L-numbers (L18, L81, L85, L177) sold as 'Gold Nugget Pleco' may represent more than one Baryancistrus species; B. xanthellus is the formally described name covering at least L18, L81, and L85. See also: fw-fish-360 (listed as Baryancistrus xanthellus) — likely a merge candidate representing the same trade complex.
breedingNotes: Rare in captivity but documented by specialist breeders. Spawning occurs in caves; males guard and fan the eggs until hatching. Conditioning requires pristine water quality, high oxygenation, and a varied diet rich in both plant matter and protein over several months. A notable temperature drop combined with increased water flow has been reported to trigger spawning in some accounts. Fry are large enough on hatching to accept crushed algae wafers and microfoods. Breeding success rate in the hobby is low and typically requires a species-only or species-focused biotope setup.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Baryancistrus xanthellus
primary.notes: Primary source for husbandry parameters, native range, water chemistry, diet, and behaviour. Covers Baryancistrus xanthellus (the formally described species encompassing L18/L81/L85), which is the primary species in the Gold Nugget trade complex.

additional.name: FishBase — Baryancistrus xanthellus
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (to 30 cm TL), taxonomy (Loricariidae, Siluriformes), native range (Rio Xingu system, Pará state, Brazil), temperature tolerance, and diet classification.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Plecostomus Care Guide
additional.notes: Trade-level care advice for loricariid plecos including Gold Nugget; confirmed diet approach (algae wafers, vegetables, protein supplements), tank size guidance, and intermediate care level classification.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Gold Nugget Pleco profile
additional.notes: UK hobbyist trade source confirming L-number synonymy (L18/L81/L85/L177), territorial behaviour, strong flow requirements, warm water needs, and supplementary protein feeding advice.

additional.name: PlanetCatfish — Baryancistrus xanthellus (L018/L085/L177)
additional.notes: Specialist catfish database; confirmed L-number taxonomy, Xingu/Tapajós origin, oxygenation requirements, breeding cave behaviour, and diet (biofilm, algae, meaty protein). Noted L81 may be a distinct population variant.

fish.breedingNotes: Cave-spawner; male guards eggs in rocky cave. Rarely achieved outside dedicated biotope setups. Requires extended conditioning on varied diet, high oxygenation, and trigger of temperature reduction plus increased flow. Fry are manageable-sized but demanding water quality needed throughout.
